title: Science: your body glows, just not where your eyes can see it
surprising_fact: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible-adjacent light all day long, but it is far too faint for human eyes to notice.
explanation:
Cells constantly run chemical reactions, and some of those reactions release ultraweak photons. Researchers have measured this faint emission, sometimes called biophoton output, and it can vary with metabolism, oxidative processes, and even time of day. This is not the same thing as the dramatic glow used in science fiction, but it is real physics happening in ordinary biology.

The memorable part is not that humans are secretly lanterns, but that living systems are noisier and stranger than our senses suggest. Reality includes signals, radiation, and patterns that are perfectly normal yet completely invisible without instruments, which is a great reminder that “I can't detect it” and “it isn't there” are not the same claim.
takeaways: Biology produces measurable light as a byproduct of normal chemistry.
Human senses reveal only a narrow slice of physical reality.
Scientific instruments often expand perception more than they merely confirm it.
